JavaScript Core Concepts (11): Statements vs Expressions — The One Everybody Mixes Up
Statements vs expressions is one of those concepts almost everyone gets fuzzy on — and honestly, that includes me too.
You can get a feel for which is which straight from the MDN docs. There you'll find what JavaScript
Expressions, on the other hand, are basically too numerous to list. The thing is, an expression usually pairs up with an operator and returns a result.

Basically, a statement doesn't return a result. Right about now you're probably thinking — wait, you just said expressions usually pair with an operator and return a result, so why isn't a variable declaration like
operator. Doesn't that make it an expression?

A lot of people get tripped up on why a "variable declaration" counts as a statement. The simplest way to think about a statement: it just sits there quietly waiting for you to call it, and its biggest characteristic is that it doesn't return any result — but
a statement always gets executed once
. The reason is that it needs to establish its lexical scope first.

, here's what's happening: at declaration time, JavaScript runs through it once because it needs to set aside a chunk of memory for that variable and map that memory to the variable name. But it doesn't return a result, which is why a variable declaration hands you back
is really just JavaScript's way of saying "I've got the memory ready for this variable."
An expression is anything that returns a value. The simplest way to see it: type

Arithmetic: evaluates to a number, for example 3.14159. (Generally uses arithmetic operators.)
String: evaluates to a string, for example "Fred" or "234". (Generally uses string operators.)
Logical: evaluates to true or false. (Often involves logical operators.)
Primary expressions: basic keywords and general expressions in JavaScript.
Left-hand-side expressions: left values are the destination of an assignment.
So an expression usually comes with some operators, but the simplest idea to hold onto is "it returns a result, or a value." So when you type
The key idea behind expressions is the operator. The equals sign is an expression — it assigns a value to
and returns the result. Which lets us circle back to why

because it's preparing a slot in memory for the variable. Now let's look at what other statements typically do:
You'll notice that anything that's a statement just returns
Expressions are the opposite — an expression always returns a result, a value.
we reach for all the time in dev are expressions too.
There's one more telltale difference between statements and expressions: a statement can't be stored in a variable, because it doesn't return a value.
A statement can't be stored in a variable
Now let's talk about another flavor of statement-vs-expression. When you declare a function, there are actually two broad ways to do it. The first is what's called a function declaration.
Basically, when you declare a function this way it doesn't return a result — same as before, it just returns
. This kind of function declaration is also called a named function declaration.
A function expression usually declares a variable, paired with an equals operator and a function. That's a function expression.
This way of declaring a function is also called an anonymous function expression. Even though typing it into Chrome still hands you back
, what's actually happening is that because this function has no
, it gets returned and stored in the
variable. The big distinguishing feature of a function expression is that it isn't affected by hoisting — so if you try to call it before the function expression, you'll get an error.
Finally, one extra special case. JavaScript has a thing called a block, which is just
. In MDN, a block is also classified as a statement, so you can write:
But if you rewrite it as an object literal, you use "
" to separate the property from its value.
The big difference between the two is that an object literal is an expression — and an expression can be stored in a variable.
A block, though, can't be — because it's a statement.
So the bottom line: if it returns a result or a value, it's an expression. If it doesn't, it's a statement.

Right — and saying it's an expression is also correct. The reason is that most of JavaScript is actually a hybrid (expression + statement). For example, we know an

If you really want to split hairs, the right way to describe it is a hybrid (expression + statement), since it has the traits of both the statement

Statements vs expressions is one of those concepts almost everyone gets fuzzy on — me included. In this post we untangle the difference: why a variable declaration counts as a statement even though it has an equals sign, why expressions always return a value, and how function declarations, function expressions, blocks and object literals fit into the picture.
